narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 48% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.50, your LSAT score of 31, your Year 1 and Year 3 grade deciles of 1 and 2 respectively, and details like your upper-middle family income and full-time enrollment plans. Among students who passed the bar, the average LSAT is notably higher at 37.27, and typical Year 1 and Year 3 deciles sit around 6, putting your early performance in the lower range compared to those who succeeded. The three biggest factors driving this prediction were your undergraduate GPA, your Year 1 decile, and your Year 3 decile. Your solid undergraduate GPA stood out positively, as it was stronger than the 3.24 average for bar passers, and it pushed the model's assessment toward expecting you to pass. In contrast, your low Year 1 decile had a strong pull in the opposite direction, signaling to the model a higher risk of bar failure based on how it compares to successful students. Similarly, your Year 3 decile reinforced that concern, contributing substantially toward the failure prediction despite your other strengths. Overall, while your background as a full-time student from an upper-middle income family aligns well with many who pass—where full-time enrollment is the norm at 93%—these academic deciles outweighed the positives in the model's eyes. This isn't a judgment on your potential, but a data-driven flag based on patterns from past students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the typical 37+ for passers, and if possible, strengthen your recent grades to climb those deciles. You might also seek targeted bar prep resources early or explore programs with supportive academic tracks. With focused improvements, you can shift these odds in your favor for future applications.
